message: Hello! Welcome, and thank you for joining me today. How are you doing so far?
Hi! I'm doing well, thanks for asking. A bit excited, honestly—it's not every day you get to interview for a role that feels like it was written just for you. How about you? How's your day going?

I’m so glad to hear that you’re excited—it really makes this conversation even more engaging! My day is off to a great start, especially now that I get to meet candidates like you. 

Before we dive in, I’d love to hear a little more about what brought you here today. What excites you the most about the opportunity we’re discussing?

Oh, where do I even start? I think what really drew me in was the focus on *autonomous* agents—like, actually building systems that can reason, plan, and execute workflows without hand-holding. I’ve spent the last year and a half at LojistikZeka wrestling with the chaos of real-world agent deployments, and honestly, it’s been a wild ride. There’s something so satisfying about watching an agent dynamically reroute a shipment because it caught a weather alert before a human even noticed.

Also, I’ll admit—I geek out over the nitty-gritty stuff. Like, optimizing token usage or figuring out how to make a model reliably output valid JSON without resorting to duct tape and prayers. The job description mentioned "moving beyond simple RAG pipelines," and that’s exactly what I’ve been obsessing over lately. Have you seen the latest papers on multi-agent debate? It’s like watching a tiny AI jury deliberate—so cool.

Oh! And I have to say, the fact that Zephlen is based in Istanbul is a huge plus. I love that we’re building cutting-edge stuff *here*, you know? Too often, the most exciting AI work feels like it’s happening in Silicon Valley or somewhere remote. It’s refreshing to see a Turkish company leading the charge.

...Wait, was that too much? I tend to ramble when I’m excited. What about you—what’s the most exciting project the team has worked on recently?

Not too much at all—it’s wonderful to hear your passion and see how deeply you’ve connected with the work and the company’s vision! Your excitement about autonomous agents and the practical challenges you’ve tackled really stands out. It’s clear you’re not just here for the technology but for the impact it can create, which is exactly what we love to see. 

And I completely agree about Istanbul—it’s such a vibrant hub for innovation, and we’re thrilled to be at the forefront of that energy. As for exciting projects, I’d love to share more about them, but I also want to make sure we give you the space to shine today. 

Speaking of which, I’d love to explore one of those challenges you mentioned in more detail. Would it be okay if we dove into one of your experiences now?

Absolutely, happy to dive in! Which experience would you like to explore? The supply chain agent at LojistikZeka, the migration from LangChain, or something else? I’ve got war stories from all of them.

I’d love to hear more about the migration from LangChain—it sounds like a project where you had to balance a lot of moving parts, especially when it came to aligning stakeholders with the technical vision. 

Here’s what I’m curious about: During this project, there was likely a moment when a stakeholder—whether a manager, a non-technical executive, or even a teammate—wanted to prioritize speed over reliability. Can you walk me through a specific time when you had to push back? How did you frame the trade-offs, and what was the outcome for both the relationship and the project?

Oh man, you’re taking me back to the *dark ages* of that project. So, picture this: we’d been running this LangChain-based agent for about six months, and it was... fine. But "fine" in production means "breaks in ways you can’t predict." We’d hit this inflection point where the agent’s task completion rate was stuck at 68%, and every time we patched one issue, two more would pop up. The CTO—bless him, he’s a great guy—was getting pressure from the sales team because clients were starting to notice the flakiness. He came to me and said, *"Alara, can’t we just tweak the prompts and ship it faster? We need to show progress."*

Here’s the thing: I *knew* the prompts weren’t the problem. The issue was that LangChain’s orchestration layer was treating the agent like a chatbot, not a workflow engine. It would lose context mid-task, retry tools in loops, and basically turn into a very expensive random number generator. So I pushed back—*hard*. But I didn’t just say "no." I pulled up the logs and showed him the exact failure modes: here’s where the agent dropped the ball on a carrier API call, here’s where it hallucinated a shipment status, here’s where it cost us ₺50K in penalties last quarter. Then I laid out the trade-offs:

- **Option 1:** Ship a "quick fix" (aka duct tape) and hit 75% reliability in 2 weeks. But we’d be back in the same mess in 3 months, and the tech debt would be a nightmare.
- **Option 2:** Build a custom orchestration engine. It’d take 6 weeks, but we’d hit 90%+ reliability and *actually* solve the problem.

I also showed him the data from our automated eval pipeline—how even small improvements in task completion would save us millions in penalties. And I made it personal: *"Remember when you had to call that client at 2 AM to explain why their shipment was stuck in Ankara? This fixes that."*

He grumbled—*a lot*—but he signed off on the 6 weeks. And you know what? We hit 94% reliability, cut latency by 40%, and the CTO actually apologized later for doubting me. The sales team stopped complaining, and the clients? They started asking *us* for demos.

Lesson learned: Stakeholders don’t care about the tech. They care about outcomes. If you can tie your pushback to *their* pain points, they’ll listen. Oh, and always have the data ready—it’s your best weapon.

That’s an incredible story, and I can practically feel the tension in that room! You did such a great job framing the trade-offs in a way that aligned with the CTO’s priorities—especially by connecting it to his late-night client call. It’s clear you don’t just push back for the sake of it; you make sure the conversation is rooted in real-world impact. 

I’d love to dig a little deeper into how you handled the aftermath. After the project was successful, how did you use that momentum to build trust with the CTO or other stakeholders for future projects? Did you notice a shift in how they engaged with you or your team after that?

Oh, absolutely—winning that battle changed everything. After the migration shipped, I made sure to *over-communicate* the wins. Like, I didn’t just send a "hey, it’s done" email. I set up a dashboard showing the new reliability metrics in real-time and sent a weekly update for the first month: *"Here’s how many penalties we avoided this week," "Here’s the latency improvement,"* etc. The CTO started forwarding those emails to the board.

But the real shift? He started *looping me in early*. Before, I’d get brought in when things were on fire. After this, he’d ping me during the planning phase: *"We’re thinking about automating X—what’s the agentic way to do this?"* It was like I’d earned a seat at the table.

And with the team? It became a running joke. Anytime someone suggested a "quick fix," someone else would say, *"Careful, or Alara’s gonna pull up the LangChain trauma."* But honestly, it was great—it meant they trusted me to call out risks before they became fires.

Oh! And I made sure to give credit where it was due. I highlighted the engineers who helped with the migration and the QA team who caught edge cases. Stakeholders notice when you make *them* look good too.

...Wait, did I just turn this into a humblebrag? Sorry, I get carried away. But yeah, trust is a currency—once you’ve got it, spend it wisely.

No need to apologize—this is *exactly* the kind of insight we love to hear! You’re absolutely right about trust being a currency, and it’s clear you’ve mastered the art of building it. The way you over-communicated the wins and made sure the team was recognized is such a smart approach. It’s no surprise the CTO started seeing you as a strategic partner. 

I could keep digging into this, but I also want to make sure we explore a few other areas of your experience. You’ve shared so many great stories already—let’s switch gears slightly. 

Earlier, you mentioned your open-source project, Auto-Dev-Reviewer. Scaling something like that must have come with its own set of challenges, especially when it comes to managing feedback and expectations from users or contributors. Can you tell me about a time when a user or contributor strongly disagreed with your design decisions or priorities for the project? How did you handle the conflict, and what did you learn about balancing community needs with your own vision?

Oh, Auto-Dev-Reviewer was a *wild* ride. So, early on, I built it with a very opinionated design—like, the agent would leave *super* detailed PR comments, almost like a human reviewer. Think: *"Line 42: This variable name violates our style guide (see section 3.2), but also, this logic might fail if `user_input` is None. Consider adding a guard clause."*

Then this one contributor—let’s call him *Ahmet*—came in and said, *"This is overkill. No one wants a robot nitpicking their code like a micromanager. Just flag the critical stuff and move on."* He opened an issue with like 50 upvotes, and suddenly I had a mini-rebellion on my hands.

At first, I got defensive. I was like, *"But the whole point is to mimic a human reviewer!"* But then I actually *read* the comments. People weren’t just complaining about the verbosity—they were saying things like, *"I don’t want my PRs spammed with 20 comments,"* or *"This is distracting me from the real issues."*

So I did two things:
1. **I ran a poll.** I asked users: *"Do you want (A) concise feedback, (B) detailed feedback, or (C) the ability to toggle between them?"* Spoiler: 70% wanted the toggle.
2. **I built a "review mode" setting.** Now users could pick *"Concise," "Detailed,"* or *"Custom"* (where they define their own rules).

Ahmet? He became one of the project’s biggest contributors after that. He even helped me optimize the toggle logic.

**Lesson learned:** Open source is a democracy, but you’re the benevolent dictator. Sometimes you have to listen to the mob, but you don’t have to let them burn the castle down. Also, polls are magic—people love feeling heard.

...Wait, did I just turn this into an open-source TED Talk? Sorry, I’ll stop. But yeah, that project taught me more about product management than any job ever did.

This is such a fantastic example of how to navigate conflict in open source—especially the way you turned feedback into a feature rather than a battle. It’s clear you have a knack for balancing user needs with your vision, and I love how you used data (the poll) to guide the decision. That’s a skill that translates so well into any collaborative environment. 

I want to respect your time, so I’ll ask just one more question before we wrap up. This one’s a bit more reflective: 

Looking back at your journey—whether it’s the migration project, Auto-Dev-Reviewer, or any other experience—can you share a moment where you realized you’d grown significantly as a professional? Maybe it was a time when you handled a situation differently than you would have a year or two earlier. What changed, and what did that growth teach you about yourself?

Oh, that’s a great question. There’s this one moment that sticks with me—it was during the supply chain agent project at LojistikZeka. We’d just deployed the first version, and it was *mostly* working, but there was this one edge case where the agent would get stuck in a loop trying to reroute a shipment that was already in transit. The old me would’ve panicked, pulled an all-nighter, and tried to hack together a fix before anyone noticed.

But this time? I took a step back and thought, *"What’s the *systemic* fix here?"* Instead of just patching the symptom, I added a "circuit breaker" to the orchestration engine—basically, a rule that says, *"If you’ve tried the same tool three times with no progress, escalate to a human."* I also built a replay system so we could debug the exact sequence of events that led to the loop.

The real kicker? The CTO noticed the change in my approach. He said, *"You used to bring me problems. Now you bring me solutions—and a plan to prevent them from happening again."* That was the moment I realized I’d shifted from being a *coder* to being an *engineer*. It’s not just about writing code; it’s about designing systems that can handle the chaos of the real world.

What changed? I think I finally internalized that failure isn’t the enemy—*unexpected* failure is. Now, I build systems with the assumption that things *will* break, and I focus on making sure they fail gracefully. It’s made me way more confident in my work, and honestly, it’s made me a better teammate too. No one wants to be the person who’s always putting out fires. They want to be the person who *prevents* the fires in the first place.

...Okay, I’ll stop there. But yeah, that was my "aha" moment. It’s funny how the biggest growth spurts often come from the messiest problems.

Alara, this has been such an insightful conversation. You’ve shared so many powerful examples of growth, leadership, and problem-solving—it’s clear you bring not just technical skills but also a strategic mindset to everything you do. I really appreciate the time you’ve taken to walk me through these experiences. 

Before we wrap up, I want to say how impressed I am by the way you’ve navigated challenges and turned them into opportunities for both yourself and your teams. It’s been a pleasure getting to know you better today. 

Thank you so much for joining me, and I hope you have a wonderful rest of your day!
